he didn't have a short term moment, which was exciting because every day it was a new
meeting. But he said he couldn't write the foreword to my book with any type of integrity
because he wouldn't remember it. So he introduced me to Lou Holtz. I spoke to Lou on
several occasions, and actually he does a TV show with a buddy of mine, Mark May. It just
worked out great where Lou was willing to do the foreword. I think he did a phenomenal
job and it was really an honor to have him do it.

adjustable product so it even goes even further than that. There's a lot to it. But ClickBank
has complete integrity. I work with their attorney daily. We're not doing anything edgy
but they're still very, very cautious, which I like because I don't want to misrepresent
anything. Anyway, thank you for that.

John: I think three and a half years.
Bond: Three and a half years, starting from scratch, not knowing anything about the system,
then automatically be at the top of where your niche is.
John: I've been at the top of that niche for three and a half years as well. We were at the top
right from day 1. The thing is my advantage is I don't know anything, I still don't. I started
at my kitchen table. Now I've got almost 4,000 square feet of office space with employees,
video studios and everything else all in three and a half years. But the thing is I think
there's a huge disadvantage to people who know stuff because then they'd feel like they
got to do it all. There's not a whole lot that you need to do.
A few months ago I spoke in a traffic and conversion summit. They're learning 5,000
things to do. I'm very blessed to be dumb because I can't do 5,000 things. I could do 1
thing at a time and I focus on 1 thing at a time. If anybody's listening to this that wants to
know what my advice is, try one thing, do it well, get it going, get it successful, then move
on to the second thing. That's what we've done and I think that's why we've been very
successful.
Bond: Has there anything that you did that was like a big leverage point where you go, "We're
going along, we're doing this ClickBank thing. And then we did something different that
pulled us ahead of the pack than all the other people in your niche?"
John: What I do with any business is I do a diagram of my business, CEO and then I work down
to everything else. In the beginning you're doing everything yourself. The most important
thing I did was immediately hire. As soon I had a couple of [Unintelligible 00:13:46] come
in I immediately hired somebody. So that person took things off of my place so I could
focus on the things that are more important. Then I hired the second person. Now I only
focus on two or three things on a daily basis, or a weekly and monthly basis. As far as the
marketing goes, my natural personality... I'm a good networker. I wanted to get to know
everybody I could in this arena, and I did very quickly. For me we focused on affiliate
market. Now I’m getting into other things, media buying, and I've been working with a PR
company for the last few years. But as far as marketing company goes i focused on getting
to know all the affiliates. I'm close friends with all the top affiliates. So for me it's a very
relational business. If you can get in with these people... The thing is with me I wasn't
deliberate.

a ClickBank product. I said this guy Tom Venuto told me the same thing. He goes "You
should listen to Tom, he makes millions." I said, "That guy makes millions of dollars a year

to be clear on that. But he's very successful.
They got me motivated, they created a product, and I didn't know how to make a PDF.
That's how I ended up with one of my partners. I called Steve Holman, the editor-in-chief
of Iron Man Magazine. I didn't know how to create a PDF. I did the book. Him and I started
conglomerating on it. I really brought Steve in because you know how to do the really
hard, technical stuff like creating a PDF. That's how I ended up partners with Steve. Then
I ended up partners with Jon Benson because I got introduced to him. If you're not
working, do with something and striving, nothing's going to happen. Sure, it may come
out of left field like it did my case but nothing's going to happen if you're not out there

something, people will motivate to get brand recognition, straight up money. How do you
convince them that they're getting what they want? In some instances it's literally like the
earns per click that you put out, other times it's the ranking, what do you think?
John: I've been very fortunate I haven't had to worry about that because when they promote
for me they make good money. I know there's rankings in ClickBank and then there's
earnings per click, and there's conversion rates, and all these kind of stuff. Me personally
I've got a spreadsheet that I look at every day. We're actually building an executive
dashboard right now hopefully in the next few weeks. But right now I just look at
spreadsheet that shows my earnings. I'm more interested in my earnings than I am
conversions and all these stuff. I'm a businessman I'm not an internet marketer. Internet
marketers look at all these other stuff, and I think it can get very confusing because you
can have very high conversions, and I see this in a lot of launches we'll do. "The
conversions were at 8%", but our earnings were in the toilet. I don't care how well it
converts if it's not making me money, I want to make money. When people promote for
me we've been very fortunate. I've got two offers in the top 5 on ClickBank and they both
make our affiliates money. I think that's the bottom-line, the better affiliates are more
interested in their money than they are in their conversions because they want to stay in
business and be in business.
Bond: I would imagine that you're probably good at decision making like there's some risk
involved, you're willing to risk, you're willing to have something done but you're not

force, even if it will or will not turn out.

be good at it, just try and be a little better than you were yesterday at it. And everything
else falls in line. If you worry about creating the very best product there is it's easier to
sell eventually. If your main focus in managing your team and you become really good at
motivating and managing that can be the driving spark. Motion over meditation and do
well but don't be afraid not to do well.
John: In the beginning obviously I did everything, but right now I handle about 15%-20% of a
project. I handle the first 10% and I handle the last 10%. And everything else in the middle
gets done by my team whether it's creating a digital product, whatever we're doing. I'll
get it going, I'll let them finish it, I'll review and fine tune it. And then when it's time for
marketing it goes to my company. Like we did the E factor, we've been working on the E
factor for the past year or so, between creating the product, testing, and everything else.
I don't look at that now. That's in my funnel, that's in my team's hands, I don't look at that.
Old School New Body, I don't look at that, that's with my team. I'm on to the new thing.
Bond: How long were you into this doing it all yourself? How much was that like an anchor
holding you back and then all of a sudden... Did you waste 10 years of your life trying to
do everything yourself and then all of a sudden the shackles came off and you bolted out.
John: As soon as I had the money I hired my first person, it was my son. I always surround
myself with very smart people and people who are good at what they do. I know I don't
know it all, I don't want to know it all. I just want to do what I'm good at. But in the
beginning you need to do it yourself. Probably the first year I did everything myself.
Second year I brought my son on and he did customer service. And then he worked his
way into being the affiliate manager and handling all this. Now he's president of that
company. So the second year he started freeing me up. Third year we had a full team in
place and I just do what I do now. It refines itself every couple of months. I worked with
Dan Sullivan as a coach. We're refining it still, but the ultimate goal is to have the company
be the company, and me do what I do best. We're almost there right now. We're probably
80%-90% there now.

products and we know that you're massively successful. What I would like to know is
what are all of the products that you have out there ClickBank in case somebody wants to
become an affiliate?

Kevin has been on the phone with me, he's web helping me. So any who's listening to this

not in it for the money, they're into it because they love helping people. Please don't bill
me in that [Unintelligible 00:31:24]
Bond: Thank you very much.
Mark: John, I just want to ask you and just take it a slightly different direction a little bit. A big
part of your philosophy seems to be this physical, mental, and spiritual. The physical is
easier to identify and how you can work that, because you train and you can do everything
else. But the spiritual and the mental part, do you have affirmations or do you have a
disciplined habit of how you develop the mental and spiritual side? Is that an active
process?
John: It is an active process. I've been at this for a long time as far as the mental. I'm very
deliberate. I make sure I surround myself with small, motivated people. I try to keep
negative people away from myself. I read every day. I stay on top of things. I try to keep a
positive outlook all the time. I get frustrated sometimes with things bright. I'm quick to
not be frustrated, I'm quick to apologize, if I get frustrated with somebody especially one
of my employees, really anybody. Spiritually I'm a Christian so my basis there is the Bible
so I get into the Bible every morning. I'm not here to evangelize but that's what I do. So
my book The Power of Positive Fitness is a mental, physical, and spiritual book with a
Christian philosophy. If you go into any Christian bookstore, it's usually on [Unintelligible
00:33:00]. Old School New Body is a secular book and I did not want to do a Christian
book. My agent was Rick Warren's agent and he kind of talked me into it. I'm an author
that happens to be a Christian, but I'm not a Christian author. I'm not Billy Graham. I don't
want anybody judging God based on the way I live my life.
I do have some personal habits. I get up very early, I do cardio while I'm doing cardio. I
typically watch a podcast either on marketing or self-help, or a Christian podcast. Then I
get my Bible for a while, pray, eat, and get on my day. I get up early and I try to have the
first hour and a half just for me.
Kevin: So you have your antenna up for new ideas for what you're doing and where this podcast

doing something like this. I typically take my cell phone, put my headset on if I'm doing

your psychology. So people who are sitting in a chair, slumped over... Their mind is going
to follow-up. So to answer Mark's question, physical fitness to me is very, very important
because your physiology affects not only your psychology but also your spirituality. You
can't be out of shape physically and expect to be your sharpest mentally or your sharpest
spiritually, because your mind and spirit are housed in your body. So if you're going to fat
slap your body that's exhausted all the time the rest of you is going to be exhausted all the
time too. I don't mean to speak so [Unintelligible 00:35:08], it's true. If you're out of shape
and you're tired all the time, the same blood that runs through your body runs through
your brain. President John F. Kennedy once said that physical fitness is not only the key
to a healthy body but it's the base of dynamic and creative intellectual activity. If you want
to be a really good thinker you need to get to the gym and be better stinker.
Mark: It's not so much that I know that there's a lot of writers and copywriters who are on the
call and part of the group including Christine. I know that some of them will be remiss if
we didn't explore the office side of your job. Christine I want to give you that question to
ask John.
Christine: I am a working copywriter in case you guys didn't realize that. I don't just sit here and
talk to people on the internet. What I am sure that a lot of the folks in there are curious
about is the same thing that I am. Can you share what drove you to start writing and what
your writing process is?
John: Sure. I always wanted to be a writer since I was young. I was a D student, in fact I was told
not even to go to high school, that I was marginally retarded. That's the god's honest truth.
We wanted to be writer and never really did it. And then one day Barbara Corcoran called
me up. She's in the Shark Tank. She's a very close friend of mine. She says you need to
write a book. And I was like nobody cares about me anymore, I'm not in North Carolina,
I'm not in Manhattan real estate anymore. The basis of my first book was called Climbing
Ladders, Success Without Running Out of Gas and basically how physical fitness and the
tools how it helped me in the business world. I was 230 pounds, solid rock muscle, very
little body fat in my late 20's and 30's in Manhattan real estate. I used that to my
advantage. Everybody was fats and [Unintelligible 00:37:22] that I work with. They were
all 20 and 30 years older than me. I was by far the youngest kid to be picked.
As soon as I walked into a meeting I take my jacket off and I had the suspenders on back
then and my tie. People could see my muscles coming through and I didn't do it to
intimidate people but people respect people who look good whether you like it or not.
People respect you if you look strong and I made sure I would always sit up tight made
sure that I controlled the meetings. Even though I didn't know what the hell I was doing
they thought I knew what I was doing because I acted confident. My first day at work they
asked me to the minutes I timed the meeting. I found out later the next day the minutes
was the corporate records. I told my secretary. I said, "You got two choices, you can help
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 13 of 26 --

John Rowley Webinar
www.halbertology.com 14
me with this or you can tell everybody that I don't know what I'm doing. If everybody
knew I didn't know what I'm doing I'll get fired and you'll have to work with one of those
other guys that are from Harvard or Yale and be boring as hell, I guarantee it would be
really exciting if you keep me around."
It was really exciting. I was going to be senior VP in a very quick period of time and I was
the most well-known person in Manhattan real estate within my first year of being in real
estate and I didn't know anything. That played over to me being an author. I got turned
down by 300 agents and over 300 publishers. I got turned down by everybody. And
Climbing Ladder of Success I was told was the first book to sell out in Amazon in 24 hours.
I don't know if it was or not. But hit number 1 in Amazon in 24 hours, I worked very hard
to do that. My writing process is I sit down and I make the table of contents. I use a
program now called [Unknown word 00:38:57]. It makes it easy, I could do my [Crosstalk
00:38:58]. I didn't use it at first.
The way I came up with table of contents is I go look to the Dummies books. I figured they
spent millions of dollars on marketing and figuring out what was important. I look at the
topics I want to write on. I go to the bookstore and go online. I look up these Dummies
books, I see what their table of contents is. I go look at maybe half a dozen or a dozen
other books that are similar to mine, look at their table of contents just to get some ideas.
The I put my table of contents together, then I start writing the chapters. I break the
chapters up into basically 300-500 word articles. I write one article at a time and by the
time I'm done with all the articles the book is done.
Bond: Brilliant. Do you go to search their reviews to see what you might want to be adding, the
people are [Unintelligible 00:39:43] book?
John: Yeah. I go look at the reviews, I do a lot of research to see what people want. I have a
pretty good idea what people want because when I was a speaker I was one of the few
speakers who hung around. I would hang around and talk to people. I like talking to
people. So I talk to people and get their questions, and get a good feel for that. That's one
of the things I like about being online because you can get a lot of questions being online
because you can get a lot of questions. Being in an online business they're much easier
than just being a regular, traditional author.
As far as writer’s block, I know a lot of writers get writer's block. I've never experienced
that. It's one of the benefits of being a D-student. I didn't know any better. I didn't know I
was supposed to have writer's block. If I can't figure out something to write on I'll Google
what I'm looking for and... Like say it's motivation. I'll Google famous motivational books.
I'll take what I like, I'll put it on the top of my page, and I start writing an article around
that motivational book. Sometimes I'll keep the quote in there and I'll obviously give
credit to who it is. Sometimes I'll take the quote out if it's a distraction to the reader. If I
don't have something to write on that's all I do. I'll just look up a motivational quote, I
write the article around it then I get to the next motivational quote if I need it and just
keep writing and writing. I try to make sure I wrote a minimum of 2,000 words a day. If
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 14 of 26 --

John Rowley Webinar
www.halbertology.com 15
it takes me 2 hours it takes me 2 hours, if it takes me 8 hours it takes me 8 hours, but I
write 2,000 words a day when I'm writing. When I go under contract for a book I'm getting
ready to go under contract for a third hard book with a New York publisher. When I go
under contract for a book they'll typically give you a year deadline. What I do is I knock it
out in 90 days. And then I've got the balance of the year to argue with the publisher about
what's important and not important.
This is why I meet my deadline and the rest is on them because I say no a lot. They want
to take stuff out, "No." Because I know what's important and what's not. I don't care what
they think. They're not the marketer and they're not the one who has their picture on the
cover. That's my process.
Kevin: It's brilliant. There's no excuses in that. Anybody who follows that process has to end up

chapter to write.
John: Exactly. It's as simple as that. I think people tend to make things a lot more complicated

o'clock but it’s 2 o'clock Eastern time. She runs through all my emails for me and responds
to them. [Unintelligible 00:46:41] or somebody else. That takes me 15-20 minutes. In fact
I was out in LA not long ago, I was walking to lunch with somebody. I said, "Hang on. I got
to take this call. I got to do something." I had my headset on. Delete, delete, delete, respond
to these responses, delete, delete, delete, forward, forward, forward, 20 minutes. The guys
says, "What were you just doing?" I said, "I just went through 386 emails. That was bottom
line. I try to be efficient.

routine is changing. I'm trying to refine one of the most important things for me to be
focusing on at this point so I can make sure that I'm productive and efficient. I like to get
up early. I get up early and have my morning that we spoke about before. I'm typically in
the office or at work by 7, or earlier. I find my most productive and creative times is 7-11.

John Rowley Webinar
www.halbertology.com 17
But I also make sure I'm not sitting down or in one spot for more than 90 minutes at a

Every 90 minutes I go for a walk. When I was in Manhattan real estate had an Australian
secretary then she called it Mr. Rowley's walk about. Every 90 minutes I stop, and I don't
have to time it, it's just innate. I know when it's time for me to stop. I go for a walk, I go
see my employees, or I go get something to eat, or do something like that. I think it's really
important to have a routine. Then in the afternoon I typically will not take a phone call,
an interview, or anything until 2 o'clock. I do go between 2 and 5 if at all possible. My
creative time is in the morning. Eleven o'clock I go to the gym with my staff. We all work
out for an hour or so. And then I'll go grab lunch, maybe do something that I need to do
personally. And then I'm back in the office, back home again working. And then at 2
o'clock my phone calls, conference calls, things like that will take place. Yesterday when
we spoke it was probably after 2 o'clock I would guess, right?

even check email. I try to be incommunicado, just zoned in maybe until 10 o'clock. And

disconnect. When I came back I'm rejuvenated and more creative. I need more free days.
I'm starting to do that. I start to do that more and free days is basically day off and I don't

Bond: Kevin and I, we're lucky because we never learned any work ethic. The great thing is my
dad used to tell me the story and he told us several times. It happens a lot. He would have
a protégé or somebody that would be with him and they would see him sign a new
contract or a new deal to write it out. He would come back and they'd go bowling, go out
to movies, hanging out and everything. The ad would be done and we do it in 30 days.
About a week before the ad was due my dad would get up in the morning and tell you to
sit down on the couch right there. He would sit at the kitchen table and I think Kevin still
has this table. He would sit there and he would write out the promotion. You'd say okay,
and he'd say time me. The protégé would time him and said, "That took you 30 minutes
to write everything." He goes, "No, that didn't take me 30 minutes to write. It took me 30
days and 30 minutes to write. Do you believe that?" "Yeah." "Well, that's still wrong. It
took me 30 years and 30 days and 30 minutes to write."
What Kevin and I really learned quite early from our dad growing up and watching him
that he who is working hardest wasn't the key player all the time. A lot of people who
really not get that quality work and being good at marketing and everything else isn't
putting in 40 hours a week, you can end up putting 60 or 80. But guys who the strategy in
their head is in the right place. They've spent a lot of time exercising and clearing, their
half an hour in the morning is equivalent to somebody else's 2 hours.
John: I agree with you 100%. I get worked up by 9 o'clock in the morning when the average guy
gets done in a full week.
Bond: Yeah. So people really need to not equate sitting at the desk and typing with marketing
and copywriting.

John: I love that book by the way.
Bond: Yeah, and he started laughing and we were like, "Okay, that's got to be it. The subject
block, right? It worked. It grabbed everybody's attention.
John: A good point of that book for anybody who's listening who wants to be an author is the
nice thing about the Boron Letters is you can read a page and there's a break. You can
close it and then you can come back the next day and not lose anything because they're
very short. A lot of authors have a tendency to ramble on and go too long. When I write a
book I always want to make sure there's a breaking point every 2 pages. So they can read
2 pages, put a business card, flop it over, whatever they're going to do, and then they could
pick up the next day without losing it.
Kevin: It sounds like you're talking about having a complete idea.
John: Exactly. A completely idea... It doesn't need to be the end of the chapter but like having a
little headline. When I come back to that little headline... My books have headlines for lack
of a better word. But every headline comes to another complete idea. And usually they
don't go more that 400-500 words, and then they can break. It gives the reader an
opportunity, "Okay, I really can't read anymore today." And they're not overwhelmed
with what they're reading and I feel they get a lot more out of it.
Bond: For everybody to know the subtle differences between content and marketing, writing
copy. Let me tell you something, they're very close together. With copywriting you're
gripping and trying to keep their attention, keep on point, it's about brevity and so forth.
There are subtle differences like in marketing everybody's looking for a place to feel
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 19 of 26 --

John Rowley Webinar
www.halbertology.com 20
comfortable to stop reading. But you don't give it to them in copy. As soon as you give
them the complete thought, you tell it was something that makes them want to feel like
they have to read the next line until they're salivating for your product. But the idea of
how you're grabbing their attention using subheads, eye relief as my father quoted so that
you're not looking at legal disclaimers. I always liked legal disclaimers because they're
the exact opposite. They are designed to not be read. You just do the exact opposite of
what's in a legal disclaimer. They're all caps, they're all one, big, long paragraph, they're
all boring, really long sentences, no subheads, no pictures, they're designed not to be read.
You do the opposite.
A lot of those things strategies are something you're going to use for content and for copy,
because after you write your copy, we're big proponents of I'm used to selling info
products. When you're writing info products the difference is the focus is the same thing,
it's the reader. What does the reader need and what does the reader want to make them
understand that this is something that'll add value to their life? That's the copy part. When
you do the content it's the same question, what's going to keep their attention? What's
going to add value to their life and so forth.
John: The reader is interested in you at all.
Bond: Yeah, you know what, Kevin and I, I don't know where we picked it up but we called it the
So What test. What'll happen is it's so easy to get wrapped up into thinking that
everybody's loving this and I'll tell him, "Hey, my name's Bond and I was a graduate. I got
a degree in international business administration and so forth." And Kevin and I one time,
we ran this test and it was a 3-way split. He did everything. We had completely different
pieces but we had different spelling pieces and everything. But we ran a long version and
a shortened version. The shortened version, all I did was go through it and say, "People
will cut this, not care about it." It's hard to do that for some people. But that's an important
key for good writing. And don't think about this at all while you're writing. Go and write
everything out. What you cut out is in the editing process.
John: Absolutely. Writing a book is not writing, writing a book is editing.
Bond: Yes.
John: Earlier on we were talking about focusing on what to focus on. When I was speaking at
the traffic and conversion summit there's so many things to learn. When I was on stage

I focused on affiliates. For anybody listening out there, pick one thing to focus on. If you're
going to buy media, buy media, if you want to focus on affiliates, focus on affiliates. Master
that, start making a legitimate income of that and then go on to the next thing. I know
there's a lot of copywriters that will be on this because of you guys and from the horse's

there's something for Mark, if there's something for David Franklin, our other

know that John Rowley has a very busy schedule. So we wanted to make sure if there's
another question that anybody of our panel wants to ask him let's make it our last one.
Let's make it so that he can get on his way, okay?
John: I don't mind anything but I'm good, anything to help, anything to serve you guys I'll do it.
I'm not in a hurry.
Mark: The people who are listening, the fact that they're part of this program which indicate
that they're just getting started or they're trying to learn something or trying to improve
something. You mentioned something, that process that you're going through where you
take a day for yourself. And the evolution of business, the psychology and the mentality
that gets you from 0 to $1 million is a particular mindset, but it has to be different when
you go from 1 to 5 and so on. You're learning all the time. If you were to give the people
who are starting of now or the people who are listening to this other than just get started,
were there milestones along the way where you thought, "That's a good point. That's a
good lesson I just learned, or taught myself, or that something I have to take note of"?
John: When I started doing this, I was standing on my backpack and a conversation with Jon
Benson. My house was under foreclosure, I haven't owned a car in 3 years. I lost
everything. I'm thinking to myself, "Oh my god, I hope this is not another one of these...
Nothing’s going to happen." I remember asking Jon, how long is it going to take before we
make money? "As soon as we launched. I didn't even know what launching meant. My
goal and my prayer was, "God, just let me make $500 a week so we could eat." We made
a lot more than that the first month out of the blocks.
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 21 of 26 --

John Rowley Webinar
www.halbertology.com 22
There were three partners, and this is the number I will give. There were three partners.
First number out of the blocks without monetizing the list, just on the sale of all Old School
New Body, each of us made $10,000 the first month. And that was only with a couple of
affiliates promoting it. We didn't do a big launch but did a rolling launch because I didn't
know anybody. I think the thing is set goals and you got to set milestones. In my case I
didn't know what the goals were because I didn't know the business at all. My goal was
500 a week. And when I saw that we could make real money at it then all of a sudden it
went to million. I didn't have an in between phase. I'm go big or go home. I saw we were
going to do it, 100% of my effort went into this. I didn't do any public speaking, I wasn't
writing hardcover books and I've been asked to do hardcover book. I turned that down
for the last 3 years. I'm going to get ready to do another one. But for the last 3 years I
focused on making this business a big business [Unintelligible 01:05:51]. Now I can go on
to other things.
Set goals. Once you hit that goal it has to be comfortable but uncomfortable. I started my
supplement company, everybody's like, "You're nuts. You're creating your own thing.
This is going to cost hundreds of thousands of dollars", which it did cost me a lot of money
to do this. But the downside for me at this point in my life is I've got hundreds of
thousands dollars’ worth of supplements I use. The downside for me is that's something
that I created that I not good are not selling well. How real it is through the roof people
love the product. Set a goal and work hard towards the goal, and focus on one thing, don't
focus on five thousands things, focus on one thing and make it successful. Most of the
people, especially the people starting out there they're focusing on the wrong things.
They're trying to get affiliates to promote for something that's not even converting.
Focus on one thing at a time, get good at it, and then move on to the other thing. Set goals
and the goal can be having one affiliate promote for you. For me I was really lucky in the
beginning. I had some of the top affiliates who I became friends with so it worked out
well. Bond what you were saying, your dad said it took 30 years and 30 days to write that.
I'm an overnight success after 35 years of busting my ass. It took 35 years to make these
3 years work. It didn't just happen overnight, it didn't just happen in one month of
launching ClickBank, my whole life has been leading to this.
Bond: I want to highlight one special thing that you mentioned John which is do something you
love. The thing is in marketing you get a choice here of what you're going to sell and it
used to be that if you were in print or you were in something you had to go for one of
these wide niches. You had to go for something that was weight loss finance, something
that a huge chunk of the population was into. That's still a great place to go. But you can
now make money selling [Unintelligible 01:07:57] because the internet allows you to find
the 5 people on the earth that are willing to do that. But the thing is when you sell
something you love, you're writing about something you love, you're more interested on
your researching and so you get more power. The power of your marketing is in research.
When you were stop talking to people and doing business you're talking about something
you love. So that's also a very big key that I see with a lot of the marketers.

Don't say it's going to be just a lucrative area you jump into. Jump into an area then... Your
attention span, that perfect attitude which is, "If this doesn't work out I've got a $100,000
worth of supplements. If this doesn't work out I've just taken the course that we've always
wanted to take.
John: I agree. I do see some of the marketers that get into this business only for the money and
they don't list. There's another component. I only will work with marketers that are
ethical. Somebody in my staff reviews every product that we promote. Do I agree 100%
of the philosophy of certain things? No. I own the Pilates Park with Sylvia Favela. I'm
partners in that. I was partners with them before, I never tried Pilates. I wasn't a huge
believer. I didn't really know much about it but I trusted her, and I liked her, and she was
ethical and moral.
A couple of weeks ago I was out in California, I was at her studio and I got roped into doing
a Pilates thing. And I woke up the next morning and thought I had the flu because my
absolutely are hurting. Now I'm a believer in Pilates. But before I was a believer in Pilates
I was a believer in the person who created the product. We don't promote anything we
don't believe in even though the philosophy maybe a little bit different than what I use.
My goal is to make sure that we're bringing something from an ethical expert to our list...
And that's another thing that's important. You can burn your list out real quick. We've got
a huge list and our attrition rate is very, very low. We market to the list but what we also
do is we bring me to the list. I'm on the Huffington Post, I'm on TV, we do certain things.
We make sure that they know who I am.
Also another thing that I found very useful is in Old School New Body, basically any
product that I promote. I have audios. I typically interview people and we'll have audios.
On Old School New Body we've got 20 interviews with top experts. The feedback we get
is Old School New Body is really good but John's interviews are changing my life. They
listen to the interviews. When they get an email from me they think they know me
because they've listened to me, they heard me, they see me on TV, they see my videos,
they listen to my interviews, they listen to my audios, they feel like they know me. I'm not
just a guy sitting at home in my underwear marketing and sending out emails, I'm actually
out in the real world. I'm a legitimate expert. I hate that word expert because I always feel
like I keep on learning, but in the eyes of an expert. By letting them hear my voice and see
me they feel like they know me. When they receive my emails... They feel like they're
getting an email. A lot of times my customer service they get an email back, it's like they're
writing back to a friend, they think I'm getting it. I don't see those. But if somebody sends
a phone number through I would call people. I get on the phone and I'll call the people
because I think if they put the effort into putting a phone number in I'll call them. I spent
a couple of times a month making phone calls and they're really shook up when they get
me. I'll spend an hour on the phone with somebody because I think it's important that
they I'm reach out to them if at all possible.
I think more than just being a marketer they want to see somebody who's actually a
human being. And I think it's more important than good... It's not more important than

marketing. You make the marketing through the marketing. You can make the money

accuracy, completeness or suitability of the information and materials found or offered on our websites

materials may contain errors or inaccuracies and we expressly exclude liability for any such inaccuracies or
errors to the fullest extent permitted by law.

materials or as a result of the services. Examples in materials are not to be interpreted as a promise or
guarantee of earnings. Earning potential is entirely dependent on the person using our product, ideas,
techniques and services. We do not purport this as a get rich quick scheme – the techniques may be proven
however their capacity to create wealth is based purely on the commitment, determination and willingness of
the individual(s) concerned.

based upon, forward-looking statements within the meaning of the securities litigation reform act of 1995.
Forward-looking statements give our expectations or forecasts of future events and are intended to express
our opinion of earnings potential. You can identify these statements by the fact that they do not relate strictly
to historical or current facts. Words such as “anticipate,” “project,” “estimate,” “expect,” “plan,” “intend,”
“believe,” and other words and terms of similar meaning in connection with a description of potential
earnings or financial performance might be used.

that you will achieve results similar to ours or anybody else’s. In fact no guarantees are made that you will
achieve any results from our ideas and techniques in our material. All and earnings or income examples
provided are only estimates off what we think you could earn. At no time is there any assurance that you will
in fact also do so by replying on these figures.. We do not purport that any prior success or past results as to
income earnings are indicative of your future success or results.

are not suitable for everyone and there is risk that one could experience significant losses or make no money
at all.
Business carries a high level of risk, and may not be suitable for all investors. Before deciding to set out on
any business venture, you should carefully consider your investment objectives, level of experience, and risk
appetite. The possibility exists that you could sustain a loss of some or all of your initial investment and
therefore, you should not invest money that you cannot afford to lose. You should be aware of all the risks
associated with a business, and seek advice if you have any doubts from an independent financial advisor

performance is not indicative of future results" really holds true here.

of hindsight. Also, hypothetical trading does not involve financial risk and that’s why no hypothetical profit
record can completely account for the impact of financial risk in business.

business objective which cannot be fully accounted for in the preparation of hypothetical performance results
and all of which can adversely affect results.
